author: @BurntKittyForge
text:
Looks good for a daydream i have on a device that would also use a bicycle freewheel sprocket , so that one direction pulls but it can slide forward again without needing to reverse ....i have thoughts on a sprocket with just rods against bearings though and another using bicycle chain flat and one sprocket but i need a big reduction for this idea...glad to see you healthy hope you have a great day sir...
edit: i like clutches lately too (in daydream stage still)  the potential, sometimes a backwards lever could be useful on clutches as in the lever backwards so you disengage the clutch by moving that lever or for start ups or also speed consistency if it's centrifugal version

author: @atrumluminarium
text: Hello Robert, a bit of an off topic question. Have you ever come across "stepped" aerofoils (aka kline-Folgeman aerofoils)? They are apparently an aerofoil shape used for RC gliders that drastically reduce skin drag at low Reynold's numbers. Might be something worth tinkering with in a future project for a low wind turbine or for the blade root of normal turbines to squeeze out a bit more efficiency.

author: @dragosniculescu6877
text: Very interesting and nice work. thank for sharing.

author: @EastBayFlipper
text:
Hi Robert, 
The 10:1 reduction gear you assembled is very interesting and having a speed reduction is grand however, does the system also produce the torque under load as well?🙂

My reason for this question is I want to assemble a logging winch/capstan out of  wood to prove the concept and help me shift some trees 🌳 😅
Without a gear reduction, a standard capstan would be impossible for one person but with a 10:1 advantage, it becomes a little more realistic and a lovely way to exercise the mind😊

author: @kadmow
text:
- I have a  2 stage 3d printed hypocycloidal drive (100:1 in <2" thick gearbox) I made back in ?? 2013 or 2014  - I left the circular pillars/pins as free floating cylinders fitted into holes on both sides of the casing - allowing replacement if they wear significantly...  (the hypocycloid does need to be fairly precise - coming up with the mathematical model for it is a trick -  in the CAD software i used, it needed to have the cartesian form - Polar is so much simpler -as it is just a sinewave wrapped around a circle.....   Back when i did it the discussions on the web was whether or not "accessible 3d printers wer eup to making them accurately enough to function well...   yep..
..
 - The implementation I did was 2 stage (without phase offset hypocycloidal elements) - it was a design curiosity for a 100:1 reducer in the most compact form I could think of - also no bearings as it was a hand cranked model...  Adding rolling element bearings at all rolling interfaces improves efficiency hugely, but of course increases the part count a lot...

 - Making things out of sheet materials with a planar cutter - does improve the ability to mas produce things rather than having to "build all the blocks" a "unit of length" at a time....  In the real world we use as much pre madebulk material as possible - usually, and only "additively" stitch things when ther eis no alternative...  (The civil construction industry really   - Is  at the origin of all additive manufacturing, but again we like to pour things in bulk lots, or insert prefabricated assemblies / materials..

author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: I have printed your split-ring harmonic drive and it was so cool to see it work, despite my terrible print settings. I printed an axle out of PLA horizontally on my 3d printer to save on time and smoothed it in a hand drill with some sandpaper and it worked beautifully. Thank you mate! I’m going to give this one a go sometime as well, seems far easier to print. Was the one you 3d printed back-driveable, Rob? How about the acrylic one? Cheers!

author: @evanbarnes9984
text: These gearboxes are also cool because they are backdriveable! Depending on your application that can be a massive benefit. I've definitely seen other designs making these from flat parts, but i think only in laser cut metal. Thanks for sharing!

author: @endeshaw1000
text: Hello. Im supporter / memebr since over 3 Years now and subscriber since even more years but watching the video updates of the last 2 Years I noticed that you totally lost the focus on chemical energy storage. The No Video at all for super capacitors and or improvements in that direction.  I cannot do it myself anymore because the graphene and ink is sold out since years. Actually I become a member because you were the only one on you tube I noticed that seriously want to takle the energy storage problem we have. We DO NOT NEED videos on how to build wind miles and Gears. That most of us can do.  Of course I appreciate the 3d printer videos but I become a memeber to support your super capacitor efforts, as this seemed to be the most non-toxic, for normal people achieve able, approach,  but that topic seem to be dead. Sorry to say this but Today I will cancel the membership due disappointment. Tons of video updates but the most interesting CONTENT like back then is missing.Why you never continued researching the degradation problem of the cells?  I keep a subscriber but as long as Energy Storgae (chemical) doesnt become, at least one of your aims again.... its just so disappointing.

replies.author: @GavinImpett
replies.text: I became a member this month and bought a 3d printer because of wind turbines and was somewhat surprised when visiting the back catalog to discover Robert's history of building batteries. I bought a commercial portable battery that suits my needs - a generator, I'm willing to put the time in to build, batteries not so much. If I ever do decide to build a battery, I can look at the back catalog. I find Robert's videos inspiring and forward thinking.

replies.author: @thesentientneuron6550
replies.text: I will say this m8, and no disrespect to you but, you do not own his channel nor does he have any obligation to cater to your particular tastes. He has addressed your points in an earlier video where other people ask him the same question about energy storage and you can view his response to that question on there. He just wants to share cool science, and you don’t seem to grasp that. Most of us, *do* indeed enjoy his gear videos, heck I’ve 3d printed a few of his gear projects and got into 3d printing in the first place because of him. I love his chemistry content, mechanics content, 3d printing content, energy storage and generation content….I love it all! One becomes a channel member not to make demands of the type of content posted but to show support and love towards the creator for *all* that they are. Rob is a man of many skills and interests, and he chooses to share it to the world. Personally, I’m grateful to him, even though I do not have the financial means to support him.

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
plus, because you can do these on a laser cutter, or 2d.. they should be pretty easy to do on a desktop cnc in stronger materials like aluminum or brass.  
I assume the shaft is the weak link in this setup, so replacing that with an aluminum or brass rod, and maybe shaving a part down for a set screw (or D-shaped cog interfaces instead of round) would pump up the durability quiet a bit.  I've found acrylic can be very very fragile when stressed in some directions, so shy away from it as a rule of thumb. 
see if @carvera can supply you with one of their machines. ;-)

author: @grendel1960a
text: it harks back to the wind and watermills where the big wooden gears just had pegs to intermesh, it made for an easy right angle gear train. another way of achieving a 10:1 reduction is through the use of a worm gear, as I recall the meccano worm gear uses a 9/16 whitworth screw thread to mesh with the other meccano gears, so a section of allthread or a bolt can be used as a worm gear with the correct mating gears, one other simple way of making gears is to use a tap,slowly feed it sideways into a brass blank whilst it rotates and it will cut you a gear to match your worm gear thread. these are just a couple of other methods to get gears, though my favorite method of getting gears is to strip down old printers, these will supply gears and nice straight precision bar stock.

replies.text: it does indeed mate - cheers
